title: Kensington Mews House
shortDescription: A complete reimagining of a Victorian mews house — preserving its original character while weaving in contemporary light-filled living spaces.
description:
This mews conversion presented a rare opportunity to blend the inherent charm of Victorian London with the spatial ambitions of contemporary living. Working closely with the clients, we stripped back decades of ill-considered alterations to reveal original brickwork, timber joists, and a graceful roof pitch — then reinterpreted these elements through a restrained modern lens.

The ground floor opens into a single flowing volume, with bespoke joinery concealing all utility spaces. A new glazed rear extension introduces a generous connection to the private courtyard garden, flooding the interior with natural light throughout the day.

Materials were selected for longevity and patina: honed limestone, aged brass hardware, and limewashed oak ensure the home will grow more beautiful with time.

heroImage.alt: Light-filled open plan living space in Kensington mews house

gallery.alt: Open plan living and dining area with limestone flooring
Bespoke kitchen with aged brass hardware and oak cabinetry
Master bedroom with limewashed oak panelling and natural light
Rear glazed extension opening onto private courtyard
Bathroom with honed limestone surfaces and freestanding bath
Staircase detail with original Victorian brickwork exposed

title: Clerkenwell Studio Offices
shortDescription: A former printworks transformed into flexible studio offices for a creative agency, retaining the raw industrial character of the original building.
description:
Clerkenwell has long been London's creative quarter, and this former printworks sits at the heart of it. Our brief was to create a workplace that inspires without overwhelming — a backdrop to focused creative work that also energises its occupants.

The existing industrial skeleton — cast-iron columns, exposed steel trusses, and original factory glazing — was preserved and celebrated. New interventions are clearly legible as contemporary additions: blackened steel mezzanines, polished concrete floors, and full-height glazed meeting pods that float within the volume.

Biophilic design principles guided the landscaping strategy; a canopy of hanging planters and a generous staff terrace ensure a daily connection to nature within this dense urban setting.

heroImage.alt: Industrial studio office with exposed steel trusses and concrete floors

gallery.alt: Main studio floor with cast-iron columns and open workspaces
Glazed meeting pod within the main industrial volume
Blackened steel mezzanine with timber flooring above main workspace
Staff terrace with planters and city views

Breakout lounge with hanging biophilic planter installation

title: Notting Hill Family Home
shortDescription: A generational family home in a Grade II listed terrace, sensitively extended and thoughtfully rearranged for modern family life.
description:
Few design problems are as nuanced as adapting a Victorian terrace for a contemporary family. Layers of previous interventions had compromised both the listed interiors and the functional flow of the home — our first task was forensic: uncovering what was original, what was added, and what was simply wrong.

The rear extension is a study in contextual contrast. A weathered steel frame and large-format glass create a room that is undeniably modern yet defers entirely to the Victorian stock brick it meets. Inside, the extension dissolves the historic boundary between house and garden.

Throughout the house, original cornicing, skirtings and shutters have been meticulously restored. New elements — kitchen, bathrooms, and joinery — are crafted to complement rather than compete.

heroImage.alt: Rear extension of Victorian terrace with weathered steel and glass

gallery.alt: Rear extension connecting Victorian terrace to garden
Drawing room with restored cornicing and shutters
Family kitchen with bespoke cabinetry and island
Master en-suite with Calacatta marble and walk-in shower
First floor landing with original Victorian staircase restored
Children's playroom in the lower ground floor conversion

title: Suffolk Heritage Barn
shortDescription: The careful conversion of a Grade II listed threshing barn into a distinctive rural home, celebrating the agricultural heritage of the Suffolk landscape.
description:
This Grade II listed threshing barn has stood in the Suffolk countryside for over three centuries. Our brief was both a privilege and a responsibility: to breathe new life into the structure as a family home, while preserving the authentic character that makes it irreplaceable.

Structural repairs were undertaken first — reinstatement of original oak pegs, replacement of decayed timbers with air-dried oak from sustainable English sources. The monumental interior volume is left entirely open, with a freestanding zinc-clad sleeping pavilion inserted within it, making explicit the conversation between old and new.

Throughout, materials are honest and unadorned: rough-sawn oak, exposed flint, rammed earth floors. The result is a home of singular atmosphere — dramatic yet deeply tranquil.

heroImage.alt: Converted Suffolk barn with original oak structure and glazed gable end

gallery.alt: Main barn interior with exposed original oak frame
Freestanding zinc sleeping pavilion within barn volume
Glazed gable end with views across Suffolk landscape
Kitchen with rammed earth floors and rough-sawn oak counters
Bathroom with exposed flint wall and freestanding bath
Exterior elevation showing original flint and timber frame

title: Chelsea Apartment — Interiors
shortDescription: A complete interior design commission for a Chelsea apartment, creating a calm and collected environment through a restrained material palette and carefully curated objects.
description:
Approached for a complete interior design commission, we worked with our clients over eighteen months to create an apartment of quiet distinction. The brief centred on creating a home that felt both timeless and personal — resistant to trend, rich with meaning.

A palette of warm white, natural linen, aged stone, and deep walnut forms the backbone of every room. Against this measured ground, carefully chosen artworks, ceramics, and textiles bring warmth and narrative. Every surface and object was selected or commissioned with deliberate intent.

Lighting design was developed in close collaboration with a specialist consultant, creating layered schemes for each room that transition gracefully from morning to evening. The result is an apartment that rewards time — the more you live in it, the more it reveals.

heroImage.alt: Chelsea apartment living room with curated objects and warm material palette

gallery.alt: Living room with linen sofas, walnut joinery and curated ceramics
Dining area with aged stone table and bespoke pendant lighting
Master bedroom in soft white and natural linen

Hallway detail with commission artwork and aged brass mirror

title: Bloomsbury Conservation
shortDescription: Meticulous restoration and reordering of a Regency townhouse within a Conservation Area, returning the building to its original integrity.
description:
This Regency townhouse in Bloomsbury had suffered severely from twentieth-century subdivisions and insensitive alterations. Our conservation approach began with extensive archival research — original plans, period photographs, and building fabric analysis — to establish an authoritative picture of the house as built.

The planning and listed buildings consent process was managed in close collaboration with the London Borough of Camden's heritage team. Our detailed Heritage Impact Assessment set out clearly how proposed works would preserve and enhance the building's special interest.

Room by room, original features were uncovered, documented and restored: a Regency ceiling rose reclaimed from beneath three layers of plasterboard; original sash windows rebuilt using traditional joinery; and a remarkable pair of tripartite chimney pieces reinstated from archival evidence. The building now stands as a model of careful stewardship.

heroImage.alt: Restored Regency townhouse façade in Bloomsbury Conservation Area

gallery.alt: Restored Regency townhouse façade in Bloomsbury
Restored first floor drawing room with reinstated ceiling rose
Original sash windows restored using traditional joinery
Reinstated Regency chimney piece with original marble surround
Lower ground floor kitchen with new contemporary interventions
Restored staircase with original ironwork balusters
